subject: general-knowledge
description: World War I & Ii MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: World War I began on?
mcqs.explanation: World War I began on 28 July 1914 when Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia, following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.

mcqs.q: World War I ended on?
mcqs.explanation: World War I ended on 11 November 1918 with Germany's surrender, a date now commemorated as Armistice Day.

mcqs.q: Who was assassinated at Sarajevo on 28 June 1914 triggering WWI?
mcqs.explanation: Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary and his wife Sophie were assassinated at Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb nationalist.

mcqs.q: Who assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand?
mcqs.explanation: Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb nationalist, assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie in Sarajevo on 28 June 1914.

mcqs.q: The Central Powers in WWI included?
mcqs.explanation: The Central Powers were Germany, Austria-Hungary, the Ottoman Empire (Turkey), and Bulgaria during World War I.

mcqs.q: The Allied Powers in WWI included?
mcqs.explanation: The Allied (Entente) Powers included Britain, France, Russia, Italy (from 1915), the United States (from 1917), Belgium, and Serbia.

mcqs.q: Italy joined which side in World War I in 1915?
mcqs.explanation: Italy originally belonged to the Triple Alliance but switched to the Allied Powers in 1915 via the Treaty of London, seeking territorial gains.

mcqs.q: The United States entered World War I in?
mcqs.explanation: The United States entered WWI in April 1917 after Germany's unrestricted submarine warfare and the Zimmermann Telegram scandal.

mcqs.q: Who was the US President during World War I?
mcqs.explanation: Woodrow Wilson served as US President (1913-1921) during World War I and presented the Fourteen Points for peace.

mcqs.q: The Hindenburg Line was Germany's major defensive system along?
mcqs.explanation: The Hindenburg Line was Germany's major defensive system built along the Aisne River in France during WWI.

mcqs.q: Mustard gas and chlorine gas were first used as chemical weapons by?
mcqs.explanation: Germany first used mustard gas and chlorine gas as chemical weapons in 1915 during World War I.

mcqs.q: The first tanks were used by Britain during?
mcqs.explanation: The first tanks in history were deployed by Britain during the Battle of the Somme in 1916.

mcqs.q: German submarines in WWI were called?
mcqs.explanation: U-boats (Unterseeboote) were German submarines that caused heavy Allied losses at sea during both World Wars.

mcqs.q: The Battle of the Marne (1914) halted Germany's advance into?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of the Marne (September 1914) halted Germany's initial advance into France, beginning trench warfare on the Western Front.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Verdun was fought in?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Verdun (1916) was one of the longest and bloodiest battles of WWI between French and German forces.

mcqs.q: The Gallipoli Campaign (1915-16) was a failed Allied attempt to?
options: Secure sea route to Russia through Dardanelles
mcqs.explanation: The Gallipoli Campaign was a failed Allied attempt to secure a sea route to Russia through the Dardanelles strait.

mcqs.q: Who became the first South Asian soldier awarded the Victoria Cross in WWI?
mcqs.explanation: Khudadad Khan became the first South Asian soldier awarded the Victoria Cross for bravery in Belgium in 1914.

mcqs.q: The most famous WWI fighter ace known as the Red Baron was?
mcqs.explanation: Manfred von Richthofen of Germany, known as the Red Baron, was the most famous fighter ace of WWI with 80 aerial victories.

mcqs.q: The Russian Revolution of 1917 led Russia to withdraw from WWI via?
mcqs.explanation: Russia formally withdrew from WWI via the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (1918) with Germany after the Bolshevik Revolution.

mcqs.q: The Arab Revolt (1916-18) was led by?
mcqs.explanation: The Arab Revolt against Ottoman rule was led by Sharif Hussein of Mecca, aided by British officer T.E. Lawrence (Lawrence of Arabia).

mcqs.q: The Balfour Declaration (1917) was issued by?
mcqs.explanation: The Balfour Declaration (2 November 1917) was a British government statement by Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour supporting a Jewish national home in Palestine.

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on?
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on 28 June 1919, formally ending World War I and imposing harsh terms on Germany.

mcqs.q: Which article of the Treaty of Versailles accepted Germany's war guilt?
mcqs.explanation: Article 231, known as the War Guilt Clause, forced Germany to accept full responsibility for starting WWI.

mcqs.q: Germany lost which territory to France under the Treaty of Versailles?
mcqs.explanation: Germany lost Alsace-Lorraine to France under the Treaty of Versailles, restoring French sovereignty over the region.

mcqs.q: The League of Nations was established in?
mcqs.explanation: The League of Nations was established in 1920, based on Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points, to prevent future wars. It was replaced by the UN in 1945.

mcqs.q: Approximately how many people died in World War I?
mcqs.explanation: Approximately 16 million people died in WWI, including 9 million soldiers and 7 million civilians.

mcqs.q: The Austro-Hungarian Empire collapsed in?
mcqs.explanation: The Austro-Hungarian Empire collapsed in 1918 after WWI, breaking into Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia and other states.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire was officially abolished in?
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Empire, which collapsed after WWI, was officially abolished in 1922 when the sultanate was ended by Mustafa Kemal Ataturk.

mcqs.q: Which battle is also known as the 'meat grinder' of WWI?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Verdun (1916) is known as the 'meat grinder' due to its massive casualties on both sides.

mcqs.q: The German plan to fight a two-front war in WWI was called?
mcqs.explanation: The Schlieffen Plan was Germany's strategic plan to quickly defeat France before fighting Russia in WWI.

mcqs.q: Trench warfare dominated which front in WWI?
mcqs.explanation: Trench warfare dominated the Western Front between France and Germany, creating a stalemate that lasted most of the war.

mcqs.q: The Zimmermann Telegram scandal involved Germany proposing an alliance with?
mcqs.explanation: The Zimmermann Telegram (1917) was a secret German proposal for Mexico to enter WWI against the USA, helping bring America into the war.

mcqs.q: World War II began on?
mcqs.explanation: World War II began on 1 September 1939 when Germany invaded Poland, prompting Britain and France to declare war two days later.

mcqs.q: Which country's invasion triggered World War II?
mcqs.explanation: Germany's invasion of Poland on 1 September 1939 triggered WWII, leading Britain and France to declare war on Germany.

mcqs.q: World War II officially ended on?
mcqs.explanation: World War II officially ended on 2 September 1945 with Japan's formal surrender aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay.

mcqs.q: The Axis Powers in WWII were?
mcqs.explanation: The Axis Powers in WWII were Germany (Hitler), Italy (Mussolini), and Japan (Emperor Hirohito / PM Tojo).

mcqs.q: The Big Four Allied Powers in WWII were?
mcqs.explanation: The Big Four Allied Powers of WWII were the USA, UK, France, and USSR.

mcqs.q: Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany in?
mcqs.explanation: Adolf Hitler was appointed Chancellor of Germany on 30 January 1933, leading the Nazi Party (NSDAP).

mcqs.q: Benito Mussolini ruled which country as Fascist dictator?
mcqs.explanation: Benito Mussolini ruled Italy as a Fascist dictator from 1922 until his execution in 1945 during WWII.

mcqs.q: Who was Japan's Emperor during World War II?
mcqs.explanation: Emperor Hirohito was Japan's monarch during World War II, reigning from 1926 to 1989.

mcqs.q: Who was Japan's Prime Minister during most of WWII?
mcqs.explanation: Hideki Tojo served as Japan's Prime Minister (1941-1944) and directed its war effort during WWII. He was executed as a war criminal in 1948.

mcqs.q: Germany's WWII strategy of 'lightning war' was called?
mcqs.explanation: Blitzkrieg ('lightning war') was Germany's WWII strategy combining tanks, aircraft, and infantry for rapid offensive warfare.

mcqs.q: The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact (1939) was signed between?
mcqs.explanation: The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact (August 1939) was a non-aggression pact between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union, with secret protocols dividing Eastern Europe.

mcqs.q: Who became UK Prime Minister in May 1940?
mcqs.explanation: Winston Churchill became UK Prime Minister in May 1940, replacing Neville Chamberlain after the failure of appeasement.

mcqs.q: Who was US President during most of World War II?
mcqs.explanation: Franklin D. Roosevelt (FDR) served as US President (1933-April 1945) during most of World War II. Harry Truman succeeded him.

mcqs.q: Who was the Viceroy of India during World War II?
mcqs.explanation: Lord Linlithgow was the Viceroy of India (1936-43), covering most of World War II before Lord Wavell replaced him.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Britain (1940) was the first major?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Britain (1940) was the first major air campaign fought entirely in the skies, between the RAF and Luftwaffe.

mcqs.q: Operation Barbarossa (1941) was Germany's invasion of?
mcqs.explanation: Operation Barbarossa (June 1941) was Nazi Germany's massive invasion of the Soviet Union, breaking the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.

mcqs.q: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on?
mcqs.explanation: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, Hawaii on 7 December 1941, destroying much of the US Pacific Fleet and bringing the USA into WWII.

mcqs.q: Pearl Harbor is located in?
mcqs.explanation: Pearl Harbor is a US naval base on the Hawaiian island of Oahu, site of the 7 December 1941 Japanese surprise attack.

mcqs.q: Nazi Germany's genocide of 6 million Jews is known as?
mcqs.explanation: The Holocaust was Nazi Germany's systematic genocide of six million European Jews and millions of others during WWII.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Stalingrad (1942-43) was a turning point on which front?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Stalingrad (1942-43) marked the turning point on the Eastern Front, with Soviet forces decisively defeating the German 6th Army.

mcqs.q: D-Day took place on?
mcqs.explanation: D-Day (6 June 1944) was the Allied invasion of Normandy, France, codenamed Operation Overlord. It began the liberation of Western Europe.

mcqs.q: D-Day was the Allied landing at?
mcqs.explanation: D-Day was the Allied amphibious landing at Normandy, France — the largest amphibious assault in history.

mcqs.q: Italy surrendered in WWII in?
mcqs.explanation: Italy surrendered in September 1943 after Mussolini's downfall. Germany then occupied northern Italy until 1945.

mcqs.q: The Pacific War saw which major battles?
mcqs.explanation: The Pacific War's major battles included Midway (1942), Iwo Jima (1945), and Okinawa (1945) between US and Japanese forces.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Midway occurred in?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Midway (June 1942) was a decisive US naval victory over Japan, a turning point in the Pacific War.

mcqs.q: The Manhattan Project was a top-secret program to develop?
mcqs.explanation: The Manhattan Project (1942-1945) was a top-secret US-led military-scientific program that developed the world's first atomic bomb.

mcqs.q: The first atomic bomb was tested at?
mcqs.explanation: The world's first atomic bomb was tested on 16 July 1945 at the Trinity Site in New Mexico (Trinity Test).

mcqs.q: The atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima was named?
mcqs.explanation: 'Little Boy' was the uranium-based atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945 by the B-29 Enola Gay.

mcqs.q: The atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki was named?
mcqs.explanation: 'Fat Man' was the plutonium-based atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki on 9 August 1945 by the B-29 Bockscar.

mcqs.q: Hiroshima was bombed on?
mcqs.explanation: The United States dropped 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945, killing tens of thousands instantly.

mcqs.q: Nagasaki was bombed on?
mcqs.explanation: The United States dropped 'Fat Man' on Nagasaki on 9 August 1945, three days after Hiroshima, forcing Japan's surrender.

mcqs.q: The B-29 bomber that dropped the Hiroshima atomic bomb was named?
mcqs.explanation: The B-29 bomber Enola Gay, piloted by Colonel Paul Tibbets, dropped 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945.

mcqs.q: Who piloted the Enola Gay over Hiroshima?
mcqs.explanation: Colonel Paul Tibbets piloted the B-29 Enola Gay that dropped the atomic bomb 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima.

mcqs.q: The B-29 bomber that dropped the Nagasaki bomb was named?
mcqs.explanation: The B-29 bomber Bockscar, piloted by Charles Sweeney, dropped 'Fat Man' on Nagasaki on 9 August 1945.

mcqs.q: Japan announced surrender on?
mcqs.explanation: Japan announced unconditional surrender on 15 August 1945, a day known as V-J Day (Victory over Japan Day).

mcqs.q: The formal WWII surrender document was signed on?
mcqs.explanation: The formal surrender document was signed aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay on 2 September 1945, officially ending WWII.

mcqs.q: Germany surrendered on?
mcqs.explanation: Germany surrendered on 8 May 1945, a day known as V-E Day (Victory in Europe Day), after Hitler's suicide on 30 April.

mcqs.q: Hitler committed suicide on?
mcqs.explanation: Adolf Hitler committed suicide in his Berlin bunker on 30 April 1945 as Soviet forces closed in on Berlin.

mcqs.q: Nazi war criminals were tried at?
mcqs.explanation: Nazi war criminals were tried at the Nuremberg Trials (1945-46) before an International Military Tribunal for crimes against humanity.

mcqs.q: The United Nations was established in?
mcqs.explanation: The United Nations (UN) was established on 24 October 1945 to replace the failed League of Nations and maintain international peace.

mcqs.q: The Bretton Woods Conference (1944) created?
mcqs.explanation: The Bretton Woods Conference (1944) created the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank for post-war global economic stability.

mcqs.q: The Atlantic Charter (1941) was signed by?
mcqs.explanation: The Atlantic Charter (August 1941) was signed by US President Franklin Roosevelt and British PM Winston Churchill, outlining post-war peace principles.

mcqs.q: The Yalta Conference took place in?
mcqs.explanation: The Yalta Conference (February 1945) between Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin shaped the post-war settlement of Europe.

mcqs.q: The Potsdam Conference was held in?
mcqs.explanation: The Potsdam Conference (July-August 1945) between Truman, Churchill/Attlee, and Stalin divided Germany and shaped post-war Europe.

mcqs.q: The Marshall Plan (1948) was designed to?
mcqs.explanation: The Marshall Plan (1948) was an American initiative to provide economic aid to rebuild war-torn Western Europe after WWII.

mcqs.q: Total WWII casualties were approximately?
mcqs.explanation: World War II casualties totaled 70-85 million (including civilians and soldiers), making it the deadliest war in human history.

mcqs.q: Which countries each suffered over 20 million deaths in WWII?
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet Union and China each suffered over 20 million deaths in WWII, the highest among any nations.

mcqs.q: India contributed how many troops to the Allied war effort?
mcqs.explanation: India contributed over 2.5 million troops to the Allied war effort — the largest volunteer army in history.

mcqs.q: Who led the Indian National Army (INA) during WWII?
mcqs.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose led the Indian National Army (INA) with Japanese support, fighting against British rule during WWII.

mcqs.q: Which island was awarded the George Cross for heroism in WWII?
mcqs.explanation: Malta Island was awarded the George Cross by King George VI for its heroism under sustained Axis bombardment during WWII.

mcqs.q: Which technology emerged from WWII research?
mcqs.explanation: WWII research produced major technological advances: radar, jet engines, penicillin, and early computers (like Colossus).

mcqs.q: The Nuremberg Trials were held in?
mcqs.explanation: The Nuremberg Trials (1945-1946) were a series of military tribunals held by the Allies to prosecute Nazi war criminals.

mcqs.q: Which country was divided after WWII into East and West?
mcqs.explanation: Germany was divided into East Germany (Soviet zone) and West Germany (Allied zones) after WWII, reunified only in 1990.

mcqs.q: Israel's creation in 1948 was a direct consequence of?
mcqs.explanation: Israel's creation in 1948 was a direct consequence of WWII (Holocaust), the 1917 Balfour Declaration, and post-war British mandate dynamics.

mcqs.q: The Cold War rivalry emerged between?
mcqs.explanation: The Cold War (1947-1991) was the ideological rivalry between the USA and USSR, direct consequence of WWII alliances breaking down.

mcqs.q: Which country did Germany invade starting WWII?
mcqs.explanation: Germany invaded Poland on 1 September 1939, triggering WWII when Britain and France declared war two days later.

mcqs.q: The League of Nations was replaced by?
mcqs.explanation: The League of Nations, established 1920, was officially replaced by the United Nations in 1945-46 after its failure to prevent WWII.

mcqs.q: The USS Missouri is where?
mcqs.explanation: Japan's formal WWII surrender was signed aboard the USS Missouri, anchored in Tokyo Bay, on 2 September 1945.

mcqs.q: Who succeeded Roosevelt as US President in April 1945?
mcqs.explanation: Harry S. Truman became US President on 12 April 1945 after FDR's death and made the decision to use atomic bombs on Japan.

mcqs.q: Which European country remained neutral during WWII?
mcqs.explanation: Switzerland remained officially neutral throughout WWII, though surrounded by Axis-controlled territory. Sweden, Spain and Portugal were also neutral.

mcqs.q: Pearl Harbor is located on which Hawaiian island?
mcqs.explanation: Pearl Harbor is located on the Hawaiian island of Oahu and serves as the headquarters of the US Pacific Fleet.

mcqs.q: Who was Supreme Allied Commander for D-Day?
mcqs.explanation: General Dwight D. Eisenhower served as Supreme Allied Commander of the Normandy invasion (D-Day), later becoming US President.

mcqs.q: The Fourteen Points were presented by Woodrow Wilson in?
mcqs.explanation: Woodrow Wilson presented the Fourteen Points to US Congress in January 1918, later influencing the Treaty of Versailles.

mcqs.q: The League of Nations was based in?
mcqs.explanation: The League of Nations was headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, and operated from 1920 until dissolution in 1946.

mcqs.q: The Armistice of 1918 was signed in?
mcqs.explanation: The WWI Armistice was signed in a railway carriage in Compiegne Forest, France on 11 November 1918.

mcqs.q: The two atomic bombings caused immediate deaths of approximately?
mcqs.explanation: The Hiroshima and Nagasaki bombings caused immediate deaths estimated at 129,000–226,000 people, with many more dying from radiation later.

mcqs.q: Japan's Pearl Harbor attack targeted the US?
mcqs.explanation: Japan's 7 December 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor targeted the US Pacific Fleet based there, aiming to cripple American naval power.

mcqs.q: Winston Churchill's famous speech 'We shall fight on the beaches' was delivered during?
mcqs.explanation: Churchill delivered 'We shall fight on the beaches' on 4 June 1940 after the Dunkirk evacuation, vowing resistance against Nazi invasion.

mcqs.q: The Holocaust was primarily carried out in?
mcqs.explanation: The Holocaust was primarily carried out in Nazi concentration and death camps, most infamously Auschwitz-Birkenau in occupied Poland.

mcqs.q: UNESCO, WHO, and ILO are associated with?
mcqs.explanation: UNESCO, WHO, and ILO became pillars of post-WWII global cooperation as specialized agencies of the United Nations system.

mcqs.q: The Holocaust's primary target was?
mcqs.explanation: The primary target of the Holocaust was European Jews — approximately 6 million were murdered. Other victims included Roma, disabled people, Soviet POWs, and Polish civilians.
